column17: Best for: Teams wanting balance of power and simplicity. Most popular project management tool.
column18: Timeline/Gantt charts paid only, automation limited on free, can get expensive for teams, learning curve for new users, reporting basic on free tier.
column19: TESTED: Used for marketing team projects. Task views (list, board, timeline) were excellent. Collaboration features (comments, assignments) worked well. However, timeline view required Premium upgrade ($24.99/user/mo). Automations were limited on free tier (only basic rules). At 15 users, would cost $375/month for Premium. Perfect for teams wanting balance of features, ClickUp is better value.

column18: Best for: Power users wanting everything in one tool. Most features but can be overwhelming.
column19: Can be overwhelming (too many features), slower performance with large workspaces, learning curve steep, mobile app limited, UI can feel cluttered.
column20: TESTED: Used for client project management. Features were incredible (could do everything in one tool). Gantt charts, mind maps, docs, goals, all included free. Automations were powerful (50+ on free). However, UI felt cluttered with so many options. Performance slowed with 1000+ tasks. New team members struggled with learning curve (3+ hours to onboard). Perfect for power users who want everything, Notion is better for simplicity.

column13: Block limit (1000 blocks on free team)
column17: Best for: Teams wanting docs + projects in one. Best for knowledge work combining docs and tasks.
column18: Not true project management (limited PM features), automation basic, no Gantt charts, no native time tracking, can get slow with large databases, mobile app can be buggy.
column19: TESTED: Used for product team (docs + tasks). All-in-one approach was brilliant (specs, tasks, decisions in one place). Database flexibility was excellent (could customize views). However, missing true PM features (no Gantt charts, no native time tracking). Automations were basic compared to ClickUp/Asana. Performance slowed with 10k+ blocks. Perfect for knowledge work combining docs and tasks, ClickUp is better for pure project management.

column16: Best for: Visual teams wanting beautiful
column17: intuitive project management. Easiest to learn.
column18: Automations charged separately (add up fast), can get expensive quickly, overkill for simple needs, less powerful than ClickUp, pricing can be confusing.
column19: TESTED: Used for creative agency projects. Visual interface was beautiful and intuitive (team learned in 1 hour). Color-coded boards and statuses made tracking easy. However, automations were charged per recipe (added up fast - spent $50/month extra on automations). At $14/user/mo for Standard, more expensive than ClickUp ($7/user/mo). Perfect for visual teams who prioritize ease of use, ClickUp is better value.

column17: Best for: Software development teams. Best for engineering teams needing issue tracking.
column18: Focused on software development (not general PM), learning curve for non-devs, limited views (no mind maps, docs basic), overkill for non-technical teams, smaller feature set.
column19: TESTED: Used for software development team. Issue tracking was excellent (sprints, backlogs, bug tracking). Keyboard-first approach was brilliant for developers. Slack integration was seamless (notifications, status updates). However, not suitable for non-technical teams (too dev-focused). Limited views compared to ClickUp. Perfect for engineering teams, Asana/ClickUp better for general project management.
